title: ACLS – Ventricular Fibrillation Cardiac Arrest
description: A witnessed in-hospital cardiac arrest presenting with ventricular fibrillation. Practise the full ACLS shockable rhythm algorithm: CPR, early defibrillation, epinephrine, amiodarone, and post-ROSC management.
learningObjectives: Recognise ventricular fibrillation on the ECG monitor
Execute the ACLS shockable rhythm algorithm (CPR → defibrillate → epinephrine → amiodarone)
Deliver a safe, correctly dosed unsynchronised shock
Administer epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3–5 minutes during pulseless arrest
Administer amiodarone 300 mg IV for refractory VFib after the third shock
Identify signs of ROSC and transition to post-cardiac-arrest care

states.prompt: A 58-year-old male collapses in the cardiac step-down unit. Staff call out – the patient is unresponsive. You arrive in seconds. Monitor shows a chaotic, high-amplitude waveform. The patient has no palpable carotid pulse. What is the FIRST action?

options.label: Start CPR immediately and call for the crash cart
Study the ECG waveform for 30 seconds before acting
Give epinephrine 1 mg IV before starting CPR
Charge the defibrillator before beginning CPR

explanations: Correct! CPR must begin immediately. Every 2-minute delay without compressions reduces survival by 10–12%.
Incorrect – waveform identification is secondary to immediate CPR when the patient is pulseless.
Incorrect – epinephrine is given after the first shock, not before beginning CPR.
Incorrect – defibrillation is important, but high-quality CPR takes priority while the defibrillator is being charged.

checklistItems.description: Immediately initiates CPR on pulseless patient

states.prompt: CPR is underway. The defibrillator pads are applied. You pause compressions for a 2-second rhythm check. The monitor shows a chaotic, high-amplitude waveform with no discernible QRS complexes. What rhythm is this?

options.label: Ventricular Fibrillation (VFib)
Ventricular Tachycardia with pulse
Asystole
Atrial Fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

explanations: Correct! VFib produces a chaotic, high-amplitude, irregular waveform with no identifiable QRS complexes.
VTach with pulse is a perfusing rhythm – this patient is pulseless. Pulseless VTach is also shockable but has a recognisable wide-complex pattern.
Asystole appears as a flat line – this waveform is not flat.
AF has identifiable (irregular) QRS complexes; the monitor shows no QRS here.

states.prompt: VFib confirmed. What energy level should you select for the FIRST biphasic defibrillation attempt?

options.label: 200 J (or manufacturer-recommended biphasic dose)
360 J monophasic equivalent

explanations: Correct! AHA 2020 guidelines recommend 120–200 J for initial biphasic defibrillation (device-specific); 200 J is the most common starting dose.
100 J is below the recommended range for initial VFib defibrillation and may be insufficient.
360 J is the monophasic equivalent; modern biphasic defibrillators are more effective at lower energies.
50 J is far too low – inadequate to terminate VFib.

checklistItems.description: Selected correct biphasic energy (120–200 J) for initial shock

states.prompt: Shock delivered. The monitor shows VFib persists. What do you do IMMEDIATELY after the shock?

options.label: Resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes before the next rhythm check
Immediately check for a pulse
Charge and deliver a second shock immediately
Give epinephrine 1 mg IV before resuming CPR

explanations: Correct! Immediate post-shock CPR (2 minutes) is mandatory. Pulse checks interrupt compressions and worsen outcomes.
Post-shock pulse checks delay compressions; resume CPR first and check after 2 minutes.
Stacking shocks is not recommended; compressions between shocks improve myocardial perfusion.
Epinephrine is important but is given during CPR, not before resuming it.

checklistItems.description: Correctly resumes CPR immediately after shock

states.prompt: Two minutes of CPR completed. Rhythm check: VFib persists. Second shock delivered. What drug should be given NOW during ongoing CPR?

options.label: Epinephrine 1 mg IV push, repeat every 3–5 min
High-dose epinephrine 5 mg IV
Amiodarone 300 mg IV (first antiarrhythmic dose)
Atropine 1 mg IV

explanations: Correct! Standard epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3–5 minutes is the first-line vasopressor for all cardiac arrest rhythms.
High-dose epinephrine (>1 mg) is not recommended and may worsen post-ROSC outcomes.
Amiodarone is the correct antiarrhythmic but is given after the THIRD shock, not the second.
Atropine is not recommended in cardiac arrest – it has no role in shockable rhythms.

checklistItems.description: Gives epinephrine 1 mg IV at correct interval

states.prompt: Third shock delivered. VFib persists (refractory). Epinephrine repeat given. What antiarrhythmic drug should now be administered?

options.label: Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus
Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV (alternative)
Magnesium 2 g IV for all refractory VFib
Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV

explanations: Correct! Amiodarone 300 mg IV is the first-line antiarrhythmic for refractory VFib/pulseless VTach after the third shock (AHA Class IIb).
Correct! Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV is an acceptable alternative when amiodarone is not available.
Magnesium is only indicated for Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VTach) — not routine refractory VFib.
Sodium bicarbonate is only indicated for specific conditions (hyperkalaemia, TCA toxicity, prolonged arrest) — not first-line here.

checklistItems.description: Administers amiodarone 300 mg or lidocaine for refractory VFib
checklistItems.evaluationHint: Patient selected ant_amio or ant_lido option

states.prompt: After the fourth shock and amiodarone, sinus rhythm restores. BP 88/54. SpO2 90%. The patient is unconscious. What is the IMMEDIATE post-ROSC priority?

options.label: Optimise oxygenation (SpO2 94–98%), support BP (MAP ≥ 65 mmHg), obtain 12-lead ECG
Hyperventilate to reduce CO2 and prevent re-arrest
Apply 100% FiO2 indefinitely to maximise oxygen delivery
Resume CPR until BP normalises spontaneously

explanations: Correct! Post-ROSC care: target SpO2 94–98% (avoid hyperoxia), maintain MAP ≥ 65 mmHg, obtain 12-lead ECG for STEMI evaluation.
Hyperventilation causes hypocapnia, cerebral vasoconstriction, and worse neurological outcomes. Target ETCO2 35–40 mmHg.
Hyperoxia (FiO2 100%) post-ROSC causes reactive oxygen species injury. Target SpO2 94–98%.
ROSC has been achieved — CPR is not needed. Continuing compressions during ROSC causes harm.

checklistItems.description: Correctly manages post-ROSC oxygenation and haemodynamics

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• VFib is a shockable rhythm – defibrillate ASAP
• CPR must resume immediately after every shock
• Epinephrine 1 mg q3–5 min; amiodarone 300 mg after the 3rd shock
• Post-ROSC: target SpO2 94–98%, MAP ≥ 65 mmHg, 12-lead ECG
